Authors: Dayuan Liu; Timo Balz

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.034

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Coherence Change Detection (CCD) is a crucial technique for monitoring surface dynamics in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), and it has wide-ranging applications in natural disaster monitoring and urban analysis. However, conventional CCD methods based on dual-temporal coherence typically rely on a single coherence estimate for decision-making. This makes it difficult to effectively distinguish between decoherence caused by real surface changes and that induced by other factors, such as geometric decorrelation, temporal decorrelation, system noise, and co-registration inaccuracies. To address this issue, this study proposes a novel dual-model framework for coherence change detection that integrates machine learning with coherence and deviation modeling. The proposed framework first employs a machine learning (ML) model for coherence estimation, followed by a deviation prediction model that learns the mapping between statistical features and estimation errors. The SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method is then applied to interpret the deviation model. Based on the joint output of coherence and deviation estimates, a dual-threshold segmentation strategy is adopted to effectively extract low-coherence regions caused by genuine surface changes. Experimental results confirm the method’s robustness in small-sample and low-coherence scenarios. Validation with real-world data across different resolutions and scenarios confirms the method’s ability to accurately identify low-coherence regions caused by changes. By jointly utilizing coherence and deviation information, the proposed framework enhances detection accuracy and interpretability, providing a robust and reliable new CCD solution.

item.title: Target-agnostic common attributes learning for few-shot semantic segmentation
item.dc:creator: Yadang Chen
Yujie Zhang
Yuhui Zheng
Zhi-Xin Yang
Enhua Wu
item.description: Few-shot semantic segmentation (FSS) aims to segment unseen categories with limited annotated support samples. Despite advancements, existing methods struggle with the issue of distribution bias. To address the problem, most existing methods try to fully exploit the target information from foreground while neglect the background. Not only that, they treat the few-shot segmentation task as a conventional target-specific segmentation problem, while we find that target-agnostic information shareable among different classes can also aid the model to rectify distribution bias. Motivated by this, this paper proposes a novel method based on a set of learnable tokens which are pretrained to learn more universal prior knowledge from both foreground and background. Specifically, during training, we leverage the learnable tokens to effectively capture target-agnostic common attributes about both foreground and background. During inference, the model leverages the support set and the learned common attributes to mitigate the distribution bias caused by the limited samples. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both accuracy and robustness. The code is available at https://github.com/Yujie32/TCANet . Published: 2026-05-22T15:34:28+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yadang Chen; Yujie Zhang; Yuhui Zheng; Zhi-Xin Yang; Enhua Wu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114032

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Few-shot semantic segmentation (FSS) aims to segment unseen categories with limited annotated support samples. Despite advancements, existing methods struggle with the issue of distribution bias. To address the problem, most existing methods try to fully exploit the target information from foreground while neglect the background. Not only that, they treat the few-shot segmentation task as a conventional target-specific segmentation problem, while we find that target-agnostic information shareable among different classes can also aid the model to rectify distribution bias. Motivated by this, this paper proposes a novel method based on a set of learnable tokens which are pretrained to learn more universal prior knowledge from both foreground and background. Specifically, during training, we leverage the learnable tokens to effectively capture target-agnostic common attributes about both foreground and background. During inference, the model leverages the support set and the learned common attributes to mitigate the distribution bias caused by the limited samples. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both accuracy and robustness. The code is available at https://github.com/Yujie32/TCANet .

item.title: TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?
item.dc:creator: Tobias Christian Nauen
Stanislav Frolov
Brian Bernhard Moser
Federico Raue
Ahmed Anwar
Andreas Dengel
item.description: The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models. Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher Published: 2026-05-21T07:36:00+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)

Authors: Tobias Christian Nauen; Stanislav Frolov; Brian Bernhard Moser; Federico Raue; Ahmed Anwar; Andreas Dengel

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models. Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher

item.title: Efficient Remote Sensing Instance Segmentation with Linear-Time State Space Distilled Visual Foundation Models
item.dc:creator: Qinzhe Yang
Keyan Chen
Jia Xu
Zhenwei Shi
Zhengxia Zou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: The computational complexity of Transformers scales quadratically with the number of tokens, which significantly constrains the efficiency of vision models, particularly recent ViT-based foundation models in dense prediction tasks. Instance segmentation, a typical dense visual prediction task in the remote sensing field, faces similar challenges. In this paper, inspired by the recent advances of knowledge distillation in large language models, we introduce RS4D - a new remote sensing instance segmentation method with linear computational complexity, which addresses the inefficiency of long sequence modeling through distilled state space modeling (SSM). We propose an adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation training method for pre-training lightweight SSM backbones, which effectively compresses knowledge from the vast self-attention space into a compact, dense linear state space. We also design a remote sensing image instance segmentation architecture based on this lightweight visual encoder, where we explore variants of three different backbones and two segmentation heads. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, including SSDD, WHU, and NWPU. Compared to ViT-based approaches, our proposed SSM backbone achieves an ×8 reduction in parameters and a ×9 reduction in FLOPs while maintaining comparable or superior accuracy to both ViT- and CNN-based instance segmentation methods. The implementation codes have been publicly available at https://github.com/QinzheYang/RS4D. Published: 2026-05-22T19:36:13+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.778 (must_read)

Authors: Qinzhe Yang; Keyan Chen; Jia Xu; Zhenwei Shi; Zhengxia Zou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3696104

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

The computational complexity of Transformers scales quadratically with the number of tokens, which significantly constrains the efficiency of vision models, particularly recent ViT-based foundation models in dense prediction tasks. Instance segmentation, a typical dense visual prediction task in the remote sensing field, faces similar challenges. In this paper, inspired by the recent advances of knowledge distillation in large language models, we introduce RS4D - a new remote sensing instance segmentation method with linear computational complexity, which addresses the inefficiency of long sequence modeling through distilled state space modeling (SSM). We propose an adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation training method for pre-training lightweight SSM backbones, which effectively compresses knowledge from the vast self-attention space into a compact, dense linear state space. We also design a remote sensing image instance segmentation architecture based on this lightweight visual encoder, where we explore variants of three different backbones and two segmentation heads. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, including SSDD, WHU, and NWPU. Compared to ViT-based approaches, our proposed SSM backbone achieves an ×8 reduction in parameters and a ×9 reduction in FLOPs while maintaining comparable or superior accuracy to both ViT- and CNN-based instance segmentation methods. The implementation codes have been publicly available at https://github.com/QinzheYang/RS4D.

item.title: Pseudo Sentences Evaluation and Quality-aware Robust Learning for Unsupervised Text-based Person Search
item.dc:creator: Kai Niu
Jiahui Chen
Ke Han
Xinyue Song
Yanning Zhang
item.description: Unsupervised Text-Based Person Search (TBPS) eliminates the need for costly manual sentence annotations by generating pseudo sentences via Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, these pseudo sentences often face the quality defect issues, resulting in semantic misalignment across modalities, which will hinder discriminative representation learning. To address this problem, we propose the PSE-QRL (Pseudo Sentences Evaluation and Quality-aware Robust Learning), a unified framework that enhances robustness to pseudo sentences for unsupervised TBPS. The PSE-QRL dynamically couples an evolving TBPS model with MLLMs to assess pseudo sentences’ reliability, and adaptively leverages high-quality ones during training. It consists of three key components: 1) Multi-granularity Sentence Augmentation, for enriching pseudo sentences with multiple granularities to broaden the diversity of image-sentence pairs; 2) Hybrid Quality Evaluation, to combine MLLM’s cross-modal reasoning knowledge with TBPS model’s person-specific distinguishing capabilities for effective sentence quality assessment; and 3) Quality-aware Robust Learning, for selecting and re-weighting samples based on quality scores to emphasize reliable sentence annotations while suppressing low-quality ones. Extensive experiments on CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PSE-QRL for improving learning robustness, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) retrieval performance for unsupervised TBPS. Published: 2026-05-22T19:37:47+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.777 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3694187

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Unsupervised Text-Based Person Search (TBPS) eliminates the need for costly manual sentence annotations by generating pseudo sentences via Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, these pseudo sentences often face the quality defect issues, resulting in semantic misalignment across modalities, which will hinder discriminative representation learning. To address this problem, we propose the PSE-QRL (Pseudo Sentences Evaluation and Quality-aware Robust Learning), a unified framework that enhances robustness to pseudo sentences for unsupervised TBPS. The PSE-QRL dynamically couples an evolving TBPS model with MLLMs to assess pseudo sentences’ reliability, and adaptively leverages high-quality ones during training. It consists of three key components: 1) Multi-granularity Sentence Augmentation, for enriching pseudo sentences with multiple granularities to broaden the diversity of image-sentence pairs; 2) Hybrid Quality Evaluation, to combine MLLM’s cross-modal reasoning knowledge with TBPS model’s person-specific distinguishing capabilities for effective sentence quality assessment; and 3) Quality-aware Robust Learning, for selecting and re-weighting samples based on quality scores to emphasize reliable sentence annotations while suppressing low-quality ones. Extensive experiments on CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PSE-QRL for improving learning robustness, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) retrieval performance for unsupervised TBPS.

item.title: Transfer graph reasoning network for misaligned visible-thermal object detection
item.dc:creator: Xiaotong Xue
Hongshu Chen
Kechen Song
Yunhui Yan
Baihua Li
Qinggang Meng
item.description: The fusion of visible and thermal images for object detection has garnered significant attention as an effective method to tackle object detection challenges in complex scenarios. However, current dual-modal fusion detection methods face two noteworthy challenges. Firstly, they often overlook the issue of registration discrepancies between visible and thermal images in practical applications. Secondly, they fail to address the gap that exists between different domain distributions, thereby directly modeling correlations within distinct domains. To address these problems, this study proposes a novel object detection method based on the fusion of visible and thermal images, accompanied by a transfer graph reasoning network. To mitigate the impact of registration discrepancies between the two modalities in practical applications, a filtering-assisted modality alignment module is developed, leveraging adaptive frequency feature adjustments to enhance the robustness of alignment. Furthermore, in order to bridge the gap between different domain distributions, this study introduces a network known as transfer graph reasoning. This network involves node transfer to compensate for semantic loss caused by registration discrepancies, as well as edge transfer to model and infer semantic relationships across different modalities. The proposed detection method has undergone comprehensive evaluations on three widely recognized datasets, namely DVTOD, LLVIP, and RGBTDronePerson. The results clearly demonstrate its outstanding performance in multispectral object detection. The code will be published at https://github.com/VDT-2048/TGRDet . Published: 2026-05-22T16:18:00+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.776 (must_read)

The fusion of visible and thermal images for object detection has garnered significant attention as an effective method to tackle object detection challenges in complex scenarios. However, current dual-modal fusion detection methods face two noteworthy challenges. Firstly, they often overlook the issue of registration discrepancies between visible and thermal images in practical applications. Secondly, they fail to address the gap that exists between different domain distributions, thereby directly modeling correlations within distinct domains. To address these problems, this study proposes a novel object detection method based on the fusion of visible and thermal images, accompanied by a transfer graph reasoning network. To mitigate the impact of registration discrepancies between the two modalities in practical applications, a filtering-assisted modality alignment module is developed, leveraging adaptive frequency feature adjustments to enhance the robustness of alignment. Furthermore, in order to bridge the gap between different domain distributions, this study introduces a network known as transfer graph reasoning. This network involves node transfer to compensate for semantic loss caused by registration discrepancies, as well as edge transfer to model and infer semantic relationships across different modalities. The proposed detection method has undergone comprehensive evaluations on three widely recognized datasets, namely DVTOD, LLVIP, and RGBTDronePerson. The results clearly demonstrate its outstanding performance in multispectral object detection. The code will be published at https://github.com/VDT-2048/TGRDet .

item.title: SpectralEarth-FM: Bringing Hyperspectral Imagery into Multimodal Earth Observation Pretraining
item.dc:creator: Nassim Ait Ali Braham
Aaron Banze
Conrad M. Albrecht
Julien Mairal
Jocelyn Chanussot
Xiao Xiang Zhu
item.description: Earth observation (EO) foundation models (FMs) are increasingly trained on multisensor data, spanning multispectral imagery (MSI), synthetic aperture radar (SAR), and derived geospatial layers, but hyperspectral imagery (HSI) remains underrepresented. Conversely, existing hyperspectral FMs are trained on HSI alone, leaving joint pretraining and fusion of HSI with co-located EO sensors unexplored. We introduce SpectralEarth-FM, a hierarchical transformer for multisensor EO input with heterogeneous spectral dimensionality. The architecture combines spectral tokenization for hyperspectral inputs, sensor-specific encoders, a cross-sensor fusion module, and a shared hierarchical encoder, enabling joint processing of HSI and lower-channel observations. To pretrain SpectralEarth-FM, we curate SpectralEarth-MM, a dataset that co-locates HSI from three spaceborne sensors (EnMAP, EMIT, DESIS) with Sentinel-2, Landsat-8/9 optical imagery, Landsat land surface temperature (LST), and Sentinel-1 SAR, over common geographic footprints. It comprises approximately 2M globally distributed locations, 25M georeferenced patches, and over 40TB of data. Pretraining uses a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA)-style objective that matches representations between global views and single-sensor local views from the same location. We evaluate SpectralEarth-FM on hyperspectral downstream tasks and standard EO benchmarks following the PANGAEA protocol, achieving state-of-the-art results across both evaluation settings. Published: 2026-05-20T12:08:16+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nassim Ait Ali Braham; Aaron Banze; Conrad M. Albrecht; Julien Mairal; Jocelyn Chanussot; Xiao Xiang Zhu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Earth observation (EO) foundation models (FMs) are increasingly trained on multisensor data, spanning multispectral imagery (MSI), synthetic aperture radar (SAR), and derived geospatial layers, but hyperspectral imagery (HSI) remains underrepresented. Conversely, existing hyperspectral FMs are trained on HSI alone, leaving joint pretraining and fusion of HSI with co-located EO sensors unexplored. We introduce SpectralEarth-FM, a hierarchical transformer for multisensor EO input with heterogeneous spectral dimensionality. The architecture combines spectral tokenization for hyperspectral inputs, sensor-specific encoders, a cross-sensor fusion module, and a shared hierarchical encoder, enabling joint processing of HSI and lower-channel observations. To pretrain SpectralEarth-FM, we curate SpectralEarth-MM, a dataset that co-locates HSI from three spaceborne sensors (EnMAP, EMIT, DESIS) with Sentinel-2, Landsat-8/9 optical imagery, Landsat land surface temperature (LST), and Sentinel-1 SAR, over common geographic footprints. It comprises approximately 2M globally distributed locations, 25M georeferenced patches, and over 40TB of data. Pretraining uses a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA)-style objective that matches representations between global views and single-sensor local views from the same location. We evaluate SpectralEarth-FM on hyperspectral downstream tasks and standard EO benchmarks following the PANGAEA protocol, achieving state-of-the-art results across both evaluation settings.

item.title: Accelerating Vision Foundation Models with Drop-in Depthwise Convolution
item.dc:creator: Carmelo Scribano
Mohammad Mahdi
Nedyalko Prisadnikov
Yuqian Fu
Giorgia Franchini
Danda Pani Paudel
Marko Bertogna
Luc Van Gool
item.description: Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available. Published: 2026-05-21T08:07:23+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Carmelo Scribano; Mohammad Mahdi; Nedyalko Prisadnikov; Yuqian Fu; Giorgia Franchini; Danda Pani Paudel; Marko Bertogna; Luc Van Gool

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available.

item.title: Supervised Classification Heads as Semantic Prototypes: Unlocking Vision-Language Alignment via Weight Recycling
item.dc:creator: David Méndez
Roberto Confalonieri
Natalia Díaz Rodríguez
item.description: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at tasks like zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval by mapping images and text to a shared space, but this requires expensive end-to-end training with massive paired datasets. Current post-hoc alignment methods reduce computational costs by connecting pretrained encoders through lightweight mappings, yet still demand substantial paired data. In this work, we investigate the potential of repurposing the classification heads of pretrained vision models as semantic prototypes. The recycling of these weights, typically discarded after pretraining, unlocks two distinct capabilities: it enables zero-shot alignment by using weights as semantic anchors, and serves as a robust data augmentation strategy by mixing these prototypes with real image-text pairs. We demonstrate that integrating our approach with several state-of-the-art post-hoc alignment techniques consistently boosts accuracy in cross-modal retrieval, zero- and few-shot classification tasks. Published: 2026-05-21T13:41:18+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: David Méndez; Roberto Confalonieri; Natalia Díaz Rodríguez

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at tasks like zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval by mapping images and text to a shared space, but this requires expensive end-to-end training with massive paired datasets. Current post-hoc alignment methods reduce computational costs by connecting pretrained encoders through lightweight mappings, yet still demand substantial paired data. In this work, we investigate the potential of repurposing the classification heads of pretrained vision models as semantic prototypes. The recycling of these weights, typically discarded after pretraining, unlocks two distinct capabilities: it enables zero-shot alignment by using weights as semantic anchors, and serves as a robust data augmentation strategy by mixing these prototypes with real image-text pairs. We demonstrate that integrating our approach with several state-of-the-art post-hoc alignment techniques consistently boosts accuracy in cross-modal retrieval, zero- and few-shot classification tasks.
